blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
If DISC tells you how you naturally lead, the Leadership Strategy Assessment tells you how effectively you are matching your leadership to what each person you lead actually needs in a specific task. This session introduces the LSA framework — drawn from situational leadership theory and contextualized through the DVULI lens of servant leadership and kingdom empowerment. The core insight is deceptively simple: there is no one 'best' leadership strategy. The best strategy is always the one that matches the development step of the person for the specific task at hand. Mismatched leadership — overseeing a master craftsperson as if they were a novice, or delegating to a novice as if they were a master — is one of the most common and costly mistakes in ministry leadership.

The LSA framework distinguishes four leadership strategies: Instructing (high directive, low support — appropriate for the Novice who is new to a task and needs clear direction), Developing (high directive, high support — appropriate for the Apprentice who is learning and needs both structure and encouragement), Mentoring (low directive, high support — appropriate for the Journeyman who has the skill but may need confidence and buy-in), and Commissioning (low directive, low support — appropriate for the Master who is fully self-reliant on the task). Participants discover both their most- and least-frequently used strategy, and are invited to examine whether they have a default approach that overrides situational awareness. The key problem this reveals is that many ministry leaders either hover over everyone (over-instructing) or release everyone prematurely (over-commissioning) — both of which produce frustration and underperformance.

The DVULI framework grounds all four strategies in SMART goal setting (Specific, Motivating, Attainable, Relevant, Time-Bound) and in the theological conviction that developing the self-reliance of those we lead is an act of servant love. A 2 Timothy 2:2 logic runs through the entire session: 'The things you have heard me say... entrust to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others.' Leadership, in this framework, is not about what you personally accomplish but about the multiplicative capacity you build in others. This session sets up the Empowerment Core Value in Session 7 and the Breakthrough Leadership definition — implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth — that participants will build toward in Sessions 15 and 16.
blocks.html:
Session 3

The Art of the Right Fit

Leadership Strategy and the Development Step — because effective leadership is never one-size-fits-all

Picture two youth workers you lead. The first is James — he arrived three weeks ago from a background in corporate training, brimming with energy and ideas, eager to run his first mid-week Bible study. He's never done it before, but his dedication is electric. The second is Maria — she has facilitated your outreach events for nine years, knows every family in the neighborhood by name, and has navigated every program challenge imaginable. But lately she comes in late, seems distracted, and leaves early. Something has shifted in her motivation since the pastoral transition eight months ago.

Now here is the question that determines whether your team thrives or fractures: Do you lead James and Maria the same way? If your instinct is to roll out a detailed orientation manual for both of them, or conversely to simply tell both "you've got this — go for it," you are doing what most ministry leaders do by default. And default leadership, as we will see in this session, is one of the most quietly costly mistakes in the kingdom enterprise.

There Is No "Best" Leadership Strategy

This is the deceptively radical claim at the heart of the Leadership Strategy Assessment (LSA) framework used throughout the DeVos Urban Leadership Initiative. It runs counter to much of the leadership literature we have absorbed over the years — literature that tends to crown one particular style as the gold standard. The LSA, grounded in situational leadership theory (Hersey, Blanchard, & Johnson, 2008), insists that leadership effectiveness is never about the leader's preferred style. It is always about the match between a leader's approach and the development level of the specific person on the specific task at hand.

That word specific carries a great deal of weight. Development level is not a personality trait or a career ranking. It is task-specific. Maria, your nine-year veteran, might be a Master at facilitating neighborhood outreach events — but if you ask her to build and manage a new social media strategy she has never touched, she may well be a Novice. James may be a complete Novice at Bible study facilitation but a Journeyman at building donor relationships because of his corporate background. The LSA framework insists that leaders resist the powerful temptation to categorize people globally and instead analyze carefully for each task (Blanchard, Zigarmi, & Zigarmi, 2013).

Research on situational leadership has consistently affirmed this task-specific logic. Graeff's (1997) extensive review of the theoretical and empirical literature on situational leadership concluded that task maturity — a follower's readiness level for a specific assignment — is the primary driver of which leadership approach produces superior outcomes. The core insight is not complicated, but its application requires ongoing attentiveness: great leaders shift their strategy to fit the person and the task, not the other way around.

Think About It

Before reading further, bring to mind one person you currently lead. Think of a specific task you have assigned them. On a gut level, how skilled and how motivated do you sense they are for that task? Now ask yourself honestly: does the way you are leading them on that task reflect a genuine assessment, or does it mostly reflect how you tend to lead everyone?

Three Skills Every LSA Leader Must Master

The DVULI workbook identifies three interlocking skills that define an effective LSA leader. These are not philosophical virtues but practical competencies — things you can learn, practice, and improve. They build on one another sequentially: you cannot match a strategy well if you have not analyzed the development step, and you cannot analyze accurately if you have not set a proper goal in the first place.

  1. Goal Setting — Coordinating what needs to be accomplished and when for those being led.
  2. Analyzing — Working with those being led to determine their skill and dedication level for a specific task they are trying to accomplish.
  3. Matching — Properly applying various leadership strategies to equip those being led with what is needed for a specific task.

Notice that this sequence is deliberate and non-negotiable. Without a clearly articulated goal, there is no common reference point for analyzing where someone is in their development on that task. And without accurate analysis, any strategy you apply is essentially guesswork, however well-intentioned (Bjerke & Renger, 2017). The DVULI framework is explicit: without first setting proper goals, an LSA leader cannot most effectively lead others.

Skill 1: Goal Setting — The SMART Foundation

Every LSA leadership conversation begins with a goal — not a vague aspiration, but a SMART goal. The DVULI framework uses the following criteria to evaluate whether a goal is strong enough to serve as the foundation for the entire LSA process:

  • Specific — What exactly is the task? What does a good job look like? (This is visible in a written goal statement.)
  • Motivating — Is this person excited and genuinely interested in this task?
  • Attainable — Is the task realistic and achievable given current resources and constraints?
  • Relevant — Is the task important and aligned with the organization's mission and goals?
  • Time-Bound — Is there a clear way to track progress? Are there measures and deadlines? (Also visible in a written goal statement.)

The empirical case for SMART goal-setting is formidable. Locke, Shaw, Saari, and Latham's (1981) landmark meta-analytic review — synthesizing over a decade of goal-setting research — established that specific, challenging goals consistently produce higher performance than vague or "do your best" goals, with goal clarity emerging as one of the three most critical moderators of performance outcomes. More recently, Bjerke and Renger (2017) demonstrated that SMART goal quality — particularly specificity and attainability — significantly moderates the relationship between leader support and follower motivation. In simple terms: a poorly constructed goal undermines even excellent leadership support.

In the DVULI context, SMART goals serve a ministry-specific purpose that goes beyond performance management. They create the clarity of expectation that respects the dignity of those we lead. Vague expectations — "be more intentional about discipleship" or "take more initiative with volunteers" — place people in an impossible position. They cannot succeed at a target they cannot see. Writing a SMART goal is an act of servant love: it gives the person you lead the gift of knowing exactly what success looks like, so they can pursue it with confidence.

The "M" for Motivating in the DVULI SMART model deserves particular attention. Traditional SMART frameworks use "Measurable," but DVULI's adaptation reframes this criterion around the follower's interior relationship to the task. Is this person genuinely energized by this work? This matters because motivation — what DVULI calls dedication — is one of the two core variables that determine a person's development step. A goal that fails to connect with what the person actually cares about is a goal that undermines the entire LSA process before it begins.

Skill 2: Analyzing the Development Step

Once a proper SMART goal is in place, the LSA leader's next task is to analyze the development step of the person being led — specifically in relation to that goal. Development step is determined by two variables operating together: Skill and Dedication.

Skill in the DVULI framework includes both proven task-specific knowledge and skills (understanding what the task requires, having done it before, being able to show examples of their work, knowing how long it takes) and proven transferable knowledge and skills (planning, interpersonal, problem-solving, and project management capabilities that may apply from other contexts).

Dedication encompasses both motivation and confidence on a specific task. Motivation includes being energized, focused, positive, and passionate about the task. Confidence includes being self-assured, independent, self-reliant, and willing to initiate. This dual nature of dedication is crucial: a person can be highly motivated but deeply lacking in confidence — as is common with excited beginners — or highly confident but disengaged — as often occurs when a highly skilled veteran loses enthusiasm for familiar work.

Taken together, the DVULI framework identifies four development steps, each representing a different combination of skill and dedication for a particular task. It is important to emphasize what the workbook makes explicit: development step is task-specific. It is not a label for a person's general capacity or value — it is a diagnostic snapshot of where they are on one particular assignment at one particular moment. A Journeyman at preaching might be a Novice at financial reporting. A Master at one-on-one mentoring might be an Apprentice at leading large group events.

The Four Development Steps

The DVULI phrase-matching exercise in the workbook captures the character of each step with precision. The four steps, from least to most developed on a given task, are as follows:

Novice New to the task · Enthusiastic · Eager to learn · Confused about the work · Inexperienced · High Dedication, Low Skill
Apprentice Learning the task · Inconsistent performance · Discouraged and frustrated · Not always confident · Some Skill, Lower Dedication
Journeyman Generally skillful · Makes productive contributions · Not always confident · Self-assured at times · Higher Skill, Variable Dedication
Master Accomplished · Consistently competent · Self-reliant · May be bored · Able to teach others · High Skill, High Dedication

The progression from Novice to Master is not linear in experience alone — it is a journey through shifting combinations of skill and dedication. The most counterintuitive step in this sequence is the Apprentice. A person at the Apprentice step typically has more skill than a Novice but has lost some of their initial excitement. They have encountered the real difficulty of the task. The early optimism of the Novice has met reality, and the result is often discouragement or self-doubt. This "disillusioned learner" dynamic is well documented in the Situational Leadership II literature (Blanchard, Zigarmi, & Zigarmi, 2013): leaders who fail to recognize the Apprentice's drop in dedication often make the fatal error of backing away ("they're learning — I'll give them space") precisely when the person most needs structured encouragement and continued direction.

The Journeyman presents a different diagnostic challenge. This is a person with genuinely developed skill — they can do the work, and do it well most of the time. But their dedication fluctuates. They may have the ability but lack the confidence to operate independently. Or they may have both ability and confidence but feel insufficiently engaged, unseen, or uncertain about the importance of their contribution. This is where Maria, our nine-year veteran, is likely operating: high skill, but motivation dimmed. The Journeyman does not need more instruction about how to do the task. What she often needs is a leader who asks her opinion, invests in her motivation, and helps her rediscover the meaning of the work she already knows how to do.

Think About It

Think about the five people on your team or volunteer roster whom you spend the most time managing. For each of them, identify one key task in their role. Without looking at a chart, what do you sense their development step is on that task? Are you more confident in diagnosing their skill level or their dedication level? What does that tell you about where your blind spots might be as an analyzer?

Skill 3: Matching — The Four LSA Strategies

With a SMART goal established and the development step accurately diagnosed, the LSA leader is ready to select and apply the appropriate leadership strategy. The DVULI framework identifies four strategies, each defined by its unique combination of directive and supportive leadership behaviors.

Directive leadership behaviors involve the leader firmly overseeing task performance through a degree of one-way communication — outlining what the requirements of the task are, and how and when things should be accomplished. The DVULI workbook lists seven specific directive behaviors: setting SMART goals, showing and telling how, establishing timelines, identifying priorities, clarifying roles, developing action plans, and monitoring and tracking performance.

Supportive leadership behaviors involve the leader drawing the person more deeply into decision-making through two-way communication — developing mutual trust and respect, which results in increased motivation and confidence. The seven specific supportive behaviors include: listening, facilitating self-reliant problem solving, asking for input, providing rationale, acknowledging and encouraging, sharing information about the organization, and sharing information about self.

The four LSA strategies combine these behavioral dimensions in different proportions, each calibrated to serve a specific development step:

Instructing: High Directive, Low Support (For the Novice)

The Novice — enthusiastic but inexperienced — needs structure, clarity, and clear direction above all else. The Instructing strategy delivers high directive behaviors (showing and telling how, establishing timelines, clarifying roles, monitoring performance) while keeping supportive behaviors relatively lower. This is not because the Novice does not need encouragement — they do — but because what primarily moves the Novice toward competence is clarity about what to do and how to do it. Excessive problem-solving dialogue with a Novice, before they have a foundation of knowledge, is often confusing and counterproductive. The Novice needs a leader who will walk alongside them closely, demonstrate competence, and provide a clear roadmap.

Developing: High Directive, High Support (For the Apprentice)

The Apprentice still needs significant direction — they have not yet achieved consistent competence — but the central challenge has shifted from confusion to discouragement. The Developing strategy maintains high directive behaviors while dramatically increasing supportive behaviors: listening actively, acknowledging effort, providing rationale for why things work the way they do, and facilitating problem-solving conversations. The Apprentice needs to feel genuinely seen and supported even while still receiving clear guidance. Dvir, Eden, Avolio, and Shamir's (2002) longitudinal field experiment on transformational leadership found that individualized consideration — tailoring leadership behavior to each follower's specific needs — was the strongest predictor of follower development. The Developing strategy is, in many ways, the most demanding of the four: it requires a leader to hold both high structure and high relational investment simultaneously.

Mentoring: Low Directive, High Support (For the Journeyman)

The Journeyman has the skills to do the work but needs the leader to invest in their confidence, motivation, and sense of ownership. The Mentoring strategy reduces directive behaviors significantly — the Journeyman already knows how to do the task and does not need to be told again — while maintaining high supportive behaviors. This means listening more than talking, asking for the person's opinion and input, facilitating their own problem-solving rather than solving problems for them, and providing meaningful rationale that reconnects them to the importance of the work. Maria, our nine-year veteran who has lost motivation since the pastoral change, is the Journeyman archetype: she does not need instruction, she needs a leadership investment in her vision and voice.

Commissioning: Low Directive, Low Support (For the Master)

The Master — self-reliant, consistently competent, and intrinsically motivated — is best served by the Commissioning strategy. Both directive and supportive behaviors are reduced, not out of indifference, but out of respect for the person's full development. The Commissioning leader trusts the Master to own the task completely: to set their own timeline, solve their own problems, and pursue the goal without being monitored or managed. The appropriate role of the leader here shifts to that of a resource — available when needed, but not hovering. Premature commissioning (releasing someone before they are ready) is just as damaging as under-commissioning (continuing to direct someone who has long since outgrown the need for direction).

Think About It

Review the sample journal entry in the DVULI workbook: "Kim kept coming to me as usual with a million questions. I was feeling frustrated that she didn't just take charge — I know she can do it. Then I realized that even though she is dedicated to the ministry… she hasn't been trained to understand why we do what we do... I've been commissioning when she needs to be mentored or developed for a while." What does this journal entry reveal about the cost of mismatched leadership — and about the kind of honest self-reflection that LSA practice requires?

The Problem of Default Leadership Styles

One of the most significant findings embedded in the LSA report — and one that consistently generates the deepest reflection in DVULI workshops — is the identification of each leader's most frequently chosen and least frequently chosen strategy. This data point illuminates a phenomenon that Blanchard, Zigarmi, and Zigarmi (2013) describe as the default style: the leadership approach a leader gravitates toward regardless of what the situation actually calls for.

Default leadership styles are not random. They tend to reflect a leader's own background, personality, and formative experiences of being led. Leaders who were shaped by highly directive environments often default to Instructing. Leaders with relational gifting and a strong desire to empower often default to Commissioning — releasing people prematurely to roles they are not yet ready to carry. Both defaults produce frustration and underperformance, but in opposite directions and for opposite reasons.

The DVULI workbook is explicit about the two most common failure patterns in ministry leadership:

"Mismatched leadership — overseeing a master craftsperson as if they were a novice, or delegating to a novice as if they were a master — is one of the most common and costly mistakes in ministry leadership."

Over-instructing — applying high directive, low support to people who are already skilled and motivated — communicates distrust, stifles initiative, and drives gifted people toward disengagement or departure. It is the leadership equivalent of hovering. Over-commissioning — applying low directive, low support to people who still need structure and encouragement — communicates abandonment. The person feels left to fail, and often does. Both are failures of love, however well-intentioned the leader.

The LSA's Matching Success metric — color-coded in each participant's report — captures exactly these mismatches. Choices rated Excellent or Good reflect appropriate strategy-step alignment. Choices rated Fair indicate a strategy one level away from optimal. Poor ratings reveal significant mismatches — a leader operating from their default rather than from diagnosis. The invitation of the LSA is not judgment but awareness: where do I tend to miss? And what might that pattern be costing the people I lead?

Dvir and colleagues' (2002) rigorous longitudinal study found that leaders trained in individualized consideration — the practice of tailoring leadership behavior to each follower's specific developmental needs — produced followers with significantly higher self-efficacy, moral development, and independent performance capacity than control groups. The LSA is, at its core, a training instrument for individualized consideration. It builds the diagnostic habit of pausing before leading and asking: Who is this person, on this task, at this moment? And what do they actually need from me right now?

The Theological Root: 2 Timothy 2:2 and the Logic of Multiplication

The DVULI framework does not treat the LSA as a neutral management tool borrowed from corporate leadership literature and applied to ministry contexts. It situates the entire framework within a specific theological conviction about the nature and purpose of Christian leadership. The anchor text is 2 Timothy 2:2:

"And the things you have heard me say in the presence of many witnesses entrust to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others."

This verse contains an embedded logic of multiplication that the LSA makes operational. Paul is not simply instructing Timothy to delegate tasks — he is articulating a vision of leadership in which the primary output is not what you accomplish but the multiplicative capacity you build in those you invest in. The key phrase is "qualified to teach others" — this is a Master-level outcome, achieved only when the leader has successfully moved people through Novice, Apprentice, and Journeyman stages by applying the right strategy at each step.

Robert Greenleaf's (2002) foundational work on servant leadership captures the same logic in secular terms. Greenleaf's "best test" of servant leadership asks whether those being served are growing as persons — becoming "healthier, wiser, freer, more autonomous, more likely themselves to become servants." This is precisely the developmental arc the LSA is designed to support. Greenleaf argues that a leader whose followers remain perpetually dependent has failed at the deepest level of leadership, however impressive the leader's own productivity. The goal is not to need to be needed — it is to work toward the point where those you lead no longer need you for the task.

Parris and Peachey's (2013) systematic review of servant leadership research across 39 empirical studies identified "developing community," "empowering others," and "cultivating follower growth" as consistent dimensions of servant leader practice, all of which are outcomes of strategic, situation-sensitive LSA application. The review further found that the multiplicative development of future leaders — what 2 Timothy 2:2 describes as training those "qualified to teach others" — is a defining feature of servant leader effectiveness.

This means that the LSA is not ultimately about managing performance. It is about loving the people you lead well enough to give them exactly what they need at each stage of their growth — even when that is uncomfortable for you. It is uncomfortable to keep directing someone who bristles at authority. It is uncomfortable to stay engaged supportively with a discouraged Apprentice who pushes back. It is uncomfortable to let go of a gifted Master who no longer needs your involvement. But each of these acts of leadership discomfort is, in the framework of servant love, an act of discipleship.

Connecting to the DVULI Empowerment Core Value

Everything discussed in this session flows directly into one of the five DVULI Core Values: Empowerment. As defined in the First National Conference curriculum, the Empowerment Core Value stresses the importance of creating "leader-full" organizations by exercising servant leadership, allowing others to assume leadership, and developing the skills of those around you. The process embedded in Empowerment is precisely the LSA sequence: Identifying → Equipping → Mobilizing → Supporting.

The LSA framework gives operational form to each step of this process. Identifying involves accurate diagnosis of development steps. Equipping is the application of the appropriate strategy — particularly Instructing and Developing for those with low to moderate skill. Mobilizing is the transition to Mentoring, where the leader steps back from direction and invests in the person's ownership and confidence. Supporting — which maps most directly onto the Commissioning strategy — is the release of a fully developed person into their own sphere of authority and responsibility.

The DVULI Breakthrough Leadership definition — implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth — is, from the LSA perspective, a Master-level outcome for those we lead. Our job as leaders is to develop the people around us until they are self-reliant enough to implement their own empowerment strategies. That is what it means to build a "leader-full" organization in the kingdom sense. That is what Paul had in mind when he wrote to Timothy from prison.

The sample journal entry in the DVULI workbook captures what it looks like when this insight lands in real ministry life: "I thought I was empowering her by expecting her to take charge, but she isn't ready. I've been commissioning when she needs to be mentored or developed for a while. I'm going to meet with her to work out a plan that will help her develop her skills so that in time she can become an even better leader." This is not just good management. This is the 2 Timothy 2:2 logic coming alive in a Wednesday afternoon conversation with a frustrated volunteer.

Think About It

Reflect on the last month of your leadership practice. For the people you lead on key tasks, which strategy did you most frequently apply — regardless of what those people actually needed? Where do you sense you defaulted? What would it take, practically and emotionally, to shift your approach to match the actual development step of each person? Consider writing a brief journal entry on this before your next workshop session.

Key Takeaways

  • There is no single "best" leadership strategy — the best strategy is always the one that matches the development step of the specific person on the specific task.
  • The three LSA leader skills are Goal Setting (SMART), Analyzing (development step), and Matching (strategy to situation) — they build sequentially and each depends on the one before it.
  • Development step is determined by Skill (task-specific and transferable knowledge and ability) plus Dedication (motivation and confidence on the task). It is always task-specific, never a global label.
  • The four development steps — Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, and Master — each call for a different strategy: Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, and Commissioning respectively.
  • Default leadership styles — over-instructing or over-commissioning — are the most common and costly failures in ministry leadership, producing frustration and underperformance in both directions.
  • SMART goal setting (Specific, Motivating, Attainable, Relevant, Time-Bound) is the non-negotiable foundation of the entire LSA process — poor goals undermine even excellent strategy selection.
  • The LSA framework is theologically grounded in servant leadership and the 2 Timothy 2:2 vision of multiplicative discipleship: our purpose is not to accomplish ministry ourselves but to develop the self-reliance of those who will carry it forward.
  • This session's framework directly prepares participants for the Empowerment Core Value (Session 7) and the Breakthrough Leadership definition that will be built toward in Sessions 15 and 16.

Looking Ahead

Session 4 will deepen the conversation about how your own DISC behavioral style — your natural patterns of dominance, influence, steadiness, and conscientiousness — shapes the way you naturally gravitate toward (and away from) certain LSA strategies. Understanding the connection between who you are and how you lead is the next essential layer in building your full leadership profile as a DVULI participant.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Four LSA strategies: Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, Commissioning and their development-step matches
Three LSA leader skills: Goal Setting (SMART), Analyzing (development step), Matching (strategy to situation)
Skill + Dedication = Development Step: the Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, Master framework
Directive vs. supportive leadership behaviors and how they combine in each strategy

items.question: What is the core insight of the LSA framework regarding leadership strategies?
items.correctAnswer: The best leadership strategy is the one that matches the development step of the person for the specific task at hand
items.correctFeedback: The LSA framework is built on the principle that no single strategy is universally best. Effective leadership requires diagnosing where a person is on a specific task and then selecting the strategy that fits that development step. This situational awareness is what distinguishes skilled leaders from those who rely on a single default approach.

distractors.text: Leaders should identify their strongest strategy and use it consistently to build trust and predictability
distractors.feedback: Students may choose this because consistency sounds like a leadership virtue. However, the LSA framework explicitly warns against having a default approach that overrides situational awareness — consistency in strategy leads to mismatched leadership.

distractors.text: Commissioning is the ideal strategy because it empowers others and reflects servant leadership
distractors.feedback: Students may gravitate toward this because empowerment is a core value in the DVULI framework. However, commissioning is only appropriate for Masters; applying it to Novices or Apprentices is a form of premature release that produces frustration and underperformance.

distractors.text: Supportive leadership behaviors are always more effective than directive behaviors in ministry contexts
distractors.feedback: Students may select this because supportive leadership feels more aligned with servant leadership values. However, the LSA framework shows that directive behavior is essential for Novices and Apprentices — the key is matching the combination of directive and supportive behaviors to the situation.

items.question: Which leadership strategy combines high directive behavior and high supportive behavior?
items.correctFeedback: The Developing strategy is the only one that is high in both directive and supportive behaviors. It is designed for the Apprentice — someone who is still learning a task and needs both clear structure (directive) and encouragement (supportive) to continue growing.

distractors.text: Mentoring
distractors.feedback: Students may confuse Mentoring with Developing because both involve high supportive behavior. However, Mentoring is low directive and high supportive, making it appropriate for Journeymen who have skill but need confidence — not the combination of both high behaviors.

distractors.text: Instructing
distractors.feedback: Students may select Instructing because it is also high in directive behavior. However, Instructing is high directive and low supportive, designed for Novices who primarily need clear direction rather than the dual emphasis on structure and encouragement.

distractors.text: Commissioning
distractors.feedback: Students may select Commissioning because it sounds like a comprehensive approach. However, Commissioning is actually low in both directive and supportive behaviors, designed for Masters who are fully self-reliant on the task.

items.question: In the DVULI framework, what does the 'M' in SMART goal setting stand for?
items.correctFeedback: In the DVULI version of SMART goals, the 'M' stands for Motivating. This reflects the framework's emphasis on goals that energize and inspire the person pursuing them, which is essential for sustaining effort in ministry leadership contexts.

distractors.text: Measurable
distractors.feedback: Students may choose Measurable because it is the most common version of the 'M' in SMART across business and management literature. However, the DVULI framework specifically uses Motivating, which reflects its focus on the internal drive of those being led.

distractors.text: Meaningful
distractors.feedback: Students may choose Meaningful because it sounds aligned with the ministry context and servant leadership values. While goals should be meaningful, the specific DVULI SMART acronym uses Motivating for the 'M' component.

distractors.text: Manageable
distractors.feedback: Students may confuse this with the 'A' (Attainable) component. Manageable relates to feasibility, but the 'M' in the DVULI SMART framework specifically stands for Motivating, emphasizing the inspirational quality of well-set goals.

items.question: A volunteer has been leading your youth Bible study for three years and consistently delivers excellent sessions with no guidance. You check in weekly with detailed lesson plans and feedback notes. What leadership problem does this scenario illustrate?
items.correctAnswer: Over-instructing a Master who should be commissioned
items.correctFeedback: This volunteer demonstrates mastery — high skill built over years and consistent self-reliance. Providing detailed plans and frequent oversight is over-instructing, which is one of the two most common and costly leadership mistakes identified in the LSA framework. A Master needs low directive and low supportive behavior (Commissioning).

distractors.text: Effectively developing an Apprentice who still needs structure
distractors.feedback: Students might view detailed oversight as good development practice. However, a three-year volunteer delivering excellent sessions independently is clearly beyond the Apprentice stage — they demonstrate the skill and self-reliance characteristic of a Master, making this level of structure unnecessary and counterproductive.

distractors.text: Appropriately mentoring a Journeyman who needs encouragement
distractors.feedback: Students might see weekly check-ins as supportive mentoring. However, the scenario describes directive behavior (detailed lesson plans, structured feedback), not primarily supportive behavior. Additionally, the volunteer's consistent excellence without guidance suggests mastery, not the Journeyman stage.

distractors.text: Under-commissioning a Novice who needs more autonomy
distractors.feedback: Students may be confused by the mismatch concept but misidentify the development step. A volunteer with three years of consistently excellent performance is not a Novice. The problem is that the leader is treating a Master as if they need Novice-level oversight.

items.question: What two factors combine to determine a person's development step for a specific task?
items.correctFeedback: The LSA framework determines development step by assessing both skill (competence on the specific task) and dedication (commitment and confidence). A person's combination of these two factors places them at one of four development steps: Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, or Master.

distractors.text: DISC profile and spiritual maturity
distractors.feedback: Students may connect the LSA to the DISC assessment covered earlier in the course. However, DISC describes natural behavioral style, not task-specific readiness. The development step is determined by skill and dedication on a particular task, not personality type or general spiritual maturity.

distractors.text: Experience and education
distractors.feedback: Students may equate experience and education with readiness because they seem like practical measures of capability. However, the LSA framework specifically uses skill (task competence) and dedication (commitment and confidence), which are more precise and situational than general experience or formal education.

distractors.text: Leadership style and willingness
distractors.feedback: Students may confuse the leader's strategy with the follower's development step. Leadership style describes what the leader does; the development step describes where the follower is. The correct factors — skill and dedication — are attributes of the person being led on the specific task.

items.question: Which development step describes a person who has strong task skills but may lack confidence or full buy-in?
items.correctFeedback: The Journeyman has developed the skill to perform the task well but may waver in dedication — lacking confidence, motivation, or full buy-in. This is why the matching strategy (Mentoring) emphasizes high support and low direction: the Journeyman doesn't need to be told how to do the task but needs encouragement and relational investment.

distractors.text: Apprentice
distractors.feedback: Students may choose Apprentice because it also involves a learning phase. However, the Apprentice still lacks full skill and needs both directive and supportive behavior (Developing). The Journeyman has the skill but faces a dedication gap — a fundamentally different need.

distractors.text: Master
distractors.feedback: Students may choose Master because of the strong task skills described. However, a Master has both high skill and high dedication — they are fully self-reliant. The Journeyman's defining characteristic is having the skill but potentially lacking the confidence or buy-in to use it independently.

distractors.text: Novice
distractors.feedback: Students may select Novice thinking the lack of confidence signals a beginner. However, the Novice lacks both skill and experience on the task. The scenario describes someone who has already developed strong skills — their challenge is dedication, not competence.

items.question: A new team member has just been assigned to coordinate event logistics for the first time. She is enthusiastic but has never done this work before. Which leadership strategy best matches her development step?
items.correctFeedback: This team member is a Novice on this specific task — she is new to event logistics coordination despite her enthusiasm. The Instructing strategy (high directive, low supportive) provides the clear, step-by-step direction a Novice needs. Her enthusiasm means she doesn't yet need high support; she needs to know what to do and how to do it.

distractors.text: Commissioning
distractors.feedback: Students may select Commissioning because the team member is enthusiastic, which might seem like readiness for independence. However, enthusiasm without skill characterizes a Novice, not a Master. Commissioning a Novice is an example of over-commissioning — one of the most common and costly leadership mistakes described in the framework.

distractors.text: Developing
distractors.feedback: Students may choose Developing because it seems like a natural fit for someone new. While Developing does include directive behavior, it also adds high support — which is designed for the Apprentice who has begun to struggle after initial enthusiasm fades. This team member hasn't yet hit that stage.

distractors.text: Mentoring
distractors.feedback: Students may choose Mentoring because it sounds relational and supportive, which feels appropriate for a new person. However, Mentoring is low directive and high supportive, designed for a Journeyman with existing skill. This team member needs clear direction first, not primarily encouragement.

items.question: According to the LSA framework, why is it a problem when a leader has one dominant default strategy they use with everyone?
items.correctAnswer: Different people at different development steps need different combinations of directive and supportive behavior, so a single approach will inevitably mismatch most situations
items.correctFeedback: The LSA reveals that leaders often have a most-frequently-used strategy that overrides their situational awareness. Since the four development steps each require a distinct combination of directive and supportive behavior, a default approach will only accidentally match the right situation — producing frustration and underperformance in most cases.

distractors.text: A default strategy signals that the leader has not yet identified their DISC personality type and needs further self-assessment
distractors.feedback: Students may connect this to the DISC assessment from the prior session. However, DISC and LSA measure different things — DISC reveals natural behavioral style, while LSA reveals strategy effectiveness. Having a default strategy is an LSA problem about situational matching, not a DISC problem about self-awareness.

distractors.text: Using one strategy repeatedly causes that strategy to lose its effectiveness over time, even with people it originally matched
distractors.feedback: Students may apply a logic of diminishing returns. However, the LSA framework does not argue that strategies wear out through repetition. The problem is mismatch — a strategy that fits one person's development step simply does not fit another's, regardless of how often it has been used.

distractors.text: Team members will perceive the leader as predictable and lose respect, undermining the leader's authority
distractors.feedback: Students may think the issue is about perception or authority. However, the LSA framework focuses on effectiveness, not impression management. The core problem is that mismatched leadership produces real frustration and underperformance — it's about developmental outcomes, not the leader's reputation.

items.question: How does 2 Timothy 2:2 connect to the theological foundation of the LSA framework?
items.correctAnswer: It establishes that leadership is about building multiplicative capacity by developing others who can in turn develop others
items.correctFeedback: 2 Timothy 2:2 — 'The things you have heard me say... entrust to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others' — provides the theological logic for the entire LSA framework. Leadership is not measured by personal accomplishment but by the self-reliance and multiplicative capacity built in those we lead. This transforms developing others from a management technique into an act of servant love.

distractors.text: It teaches that leaders should commission all believers immediately because the urgency of ministry demands rapid delegation
distractors.feedback: Students may connect the passage's emphasis on entrusting to others with the Commissioning strategy. However, the verse specifies 'reliable people who will also be qualified' — implying a development process. The LSA framework insists that premature commissioning is as harmful as over-instructing.

distractors.text: It instructs leaders to use only the Instructing strategy because Paul was directly teaching Timothy what to say
distractors.feedback: Students may focus on the teaching dynamic in the verse and equate it with the Instructing strategy. However, Paul's relationship with Timothy was mature and multifaceted — the verse's deeper point is about multiplication across generations of leaders, which requires moving people through all four development steps.

distractors.text: It demonstrates that spiritual authority should flow in a top-down hierarchy from senior leaders to newer believers
distractors.feedback: Students may interpret the generational chain in the verse as a hierarchical structure. However, the DVULI framework reads it through the lens of servant leadership and kingdom empowerment — the goal is developing self-reliance in others, not maintaining positional authority.

items.question: A ministry leader notices that a volunteer who was once highly competent at leading small groups has recently become hesitant and disengaged after a difficult group conflict. The leader responds by giving the volunteer detailed scripts and step-by-step instructions for each session. What is wrong with this approach?
items.correctAnswer: The volunteer's challenge is dedication, not skill, so the leader should use Mentoring with high support and low direction rather than Instructing
items.correctFeedback: This volunteer has demonstrated skill in small group leadership but is experiencing a dedication dip — likely in confidence — after a difficult experience. This places them at the Journeyman development step. The Instructing strategy (high directive, low support) addresses a skill gap that doesn't exist here and may communicate distrust. The Mentoring strategy (low directive, high support) would address the real need: rebuilding confidence and buy-in.

distractors.text: The leader should use the Commissioning strategy since the volunteer has previously proven their mastery of small group leadership
distractors.feedback: Students may reason that past competence equals mastery. However, the LSA framework assesses development step on the current task situation, not historical performance. The volunteer's hesitancy and disengagement signal a dedication drop, meaning they are no longer functioning as a Master and need more support — not less involvement from the leader.

distractors.text: The leader should use the Developing strategy since the volunteer now needs both high direction to rebuild skills and high support for encouragement
distractors.feedback: Students may see the volunteer's struggles and conclude that both directive and supportive behavior are needed. However, the volunteer's skill has not actually diminished — a conflict shook their confidence, not their competence. The Developing strategy's high directive component would be addressing a problem (skill deficit) that doesn't exist.

distractors.text: The Instructing approach is actually correct because the volunteer's disengagement indicates they have regressed to the Novice development step
distractors.feedback: Students may interpret disengagement as evidence of regression to Novice status. However, the LSA framework distinguishes between skill and dedication. The volunteer still possesses the skills; their challenge is motivational and emotional. Treating a skilled but discouraged person like a Novice can deepen their disengagement by signaling distrust of abilities they still have.

questions.stem: In the LSA framework, which leadership strategy is most appropriate for a Novice — someone who is brand new to a task and has low skill but may have high initial enthusiasm?
options: Instructing (high directive, low supportive)
Developing (high directive, high supportive)
Mentoring (low directive, high supportive)
Commissioning (low directive, low supportive)

feedback.correct: Instructing is the right match for the Novice. Because the person lacks skill on this specific task, they need clear, step-by-step direction — not encouragement to figure it out on their own. High directive behavior provides the structure a Novice needs to build competence.
feedback.incorrect: A common error is thinking a Novice needs lots of encouragement (high support). While encouragement isn't harmful, the Novice's primary need is clear direction because they lack the skill to perform the task. The Instructing strategy — high directive, low supportive — is the correct match.

variants.stem: A volunteer has never organized a community event before and is about to plan their first one. According to the LSA framework, which leadership strategy should their leader use?
options: Instructing — provide clear, specific directions for each step of planning
Commissioning — trust them to figure it out independently
Mentoring — offer encouragement and let them set their own approach
Developing — give them a partner and let the two of them work it out together

feedback.correct: Instructing is correct because this volunteer is a Novice on the task of event planning. They need high directive behavior — clear steps, defined expectations, and close oversight — to build competence. Letting them figure it out alone would be over-commissioning a Novice.
feedback.incorrect: When someone has never performed a task before, their primary need is structure and direction, not autonomy. The LSA framework matches Instructing (high directive, low supportive) to the Novice step because skill must be built before independence can be granted.

questions.stem: In the LSA framework, a person's development step on any given task is determined by which two factors?
options: Skill and Dedication
Personality and Experience
Calling and Character
Talent and Motivation

feedback.correct: The LSA model defines the development step as the combination of Skill (competence on the specific task) and Dedication (commitment and confidence toward the task). Together these two factors determine whether someone is a Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, or Master on a given task.
feedback.incorrect: While personality and experience matter in leadership broadly, the LSA framework specifically uses Skill and Dedication as the two-factor formula for determining a person's development step. This is task-specific — the same person can be at different development steps on different tasks.

variants.stem: According to the LSA model, what two components combine to place a person at one of the four development steps (Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, Master)?
options: Skill and Dedication
Directive behavior and Supportive behavior
Authenticity and Teachability
DISC style and Ministry experience

feedback.correct: Skill and Dedication are the two ingredients that define the development step. Skill refers to task-specific competence, while Dedication captures the person's commitment and confidence. These are assessed for each specific task, not as global traits.
feedback.incorrect: Directive and supportive behaviors describe what the leader does, not the follower's readiness. Authenticity and Teachability come from the Personal Assessment Model in Session 1. The development step is determined by the follower's Skill and Dedication on a specific task.

questions.stem: A youth pastor assigns a brand-new teenage intern to independently run the entire Wednesday night program with no guidance, check-ins, or structure. According to the LSA framework, what is the most likely result?
options: The intern becomes frustrated and the program suffers due to over-commissioning
The intern rises to the challenge and quickly develops mastery
The intern performs well because young people are naturally creative
The intern develops at the same rate as they would with any other strategy
justifications: Commissioning (low directive, low supportive) is designed for Masters who are self-reliant on a task; using it with a Novice denies them the structured direction they need, producing confusion and failure
Over-commissioning works poorly because new people always lack motivation and need emotional support before anything else
The problem is that Commissioning provides too much supportive behavior, which overwhelms a beginner
Any strategy works poorly with teenagers specifically, because they always need high directive leadership regardless of skill level

feedback.correct: This is a classic case of over-commissioning — treating a Novice as if they were a Master. The intern lacks the skill to run the program independently, so they need Instructing (high directive, low supportive) to build competence. Releasing someone prematurely is one of the most common and costly leadership mistakes in ministry.
feedback.incorrect: The LSA framework warns that delegating full autonomy to someone who is new to a task leads to frustration and failure. This is called over-commissioning. The intern is a Novice on this task and needs clear direction (Instructing strategy), not independence.
feedback.wrongReason: Some students correctly identify over-commissioning as the problem but misunderstand WHY it fails. It's not about lacking motivation or receiving too much support — it's specifically about the absence of directive behavior that a Novice needs to develop skill. Commissioning is actually LOW in both directive and supportive behavior.

variants.stem: A ministry director tells a newly hired assistant — who has never managed a budget before — 'Here's the login to our finances. I trust your judgment completely. Handle it however you see fit.' What outcome does the LSA framework predict?
options: The assistant will likely struggle and make costly errors due to a strategy-step mismatch
The assistant will thrive because trust is the most important leadership gift
The assistant will perform adequately since budget management is straightforward
The assistant will immediately seek out their own training resources
justifications: The assistant is a Novice at budget management and needs Instructing (high directive) — providing full autonomy through Commissioning creates a dangerous mismatch between the person's development step and the leader's strategy
The mismatch occurs because the assistant has the wrong DISC style for financial management tasks
Commissioning fails here because it provides too much emotional support, making the assistant overconfident
The mismatch would be resolved if the director simply set a SMART goal, since goal clarity replaces the need for strategy matching

feedback.correct: This is over-commissioning a Novice. The assistant has no skill in budget management and needs the high directive behavior of the Instructing strategy — clear procedures, defined limits, and close oversight. Trusting someone's judgment on a task they've never performed isn't empowerment; it's abandonment.
feedback.incorrect: While trust matters in leadership, the LSA framework teaches that trust must be calibrated to the person's development step on a specific task. Giving full autonomy to a budget Novice risks real harm. The correct strategy is Instructing: high direction, low support.
feedback.wrongReason: Students sometimes correctly identify the mismatch but attribute it to personality factors (like DISC) or believe that SMART goals alone can fix the problem. The core issue is specifically that Commissioning denies the Novice the directive behavior they need to build competence.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The Developing leadership strategy is the only one of the four LSA strategies that combines high directive AND high supportive behavior.
questions.reason: The Apprentice development step describes someone who is building skill but experiencing wavering dedication, so they need both structure and encouragement simultaneously.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion. The Apprentice is gaining skill but often hits a wall of frustration or self-doubt (wavering dedication). The Developing strategy uniquely combines clear direction (to continue building skill) with high support (to sustain motivation through the difficult learning curve). The strategy design flows directly from the Apprentice's dual need.
feedback.incorrect: Check each statement independently first. The Developing strategy IS the only strategy high in both dimensions (Instructing is high directive/low supportive; Mentoring is low directive/high supportive; Commissioning is low in both). The Apprentice DOES need both structure and encouragement. And the reason DOES explain why Developing is designed the way it is — the strategy matches the step.

variants.assertion: The Mentoring strategy uses low directive behavior despite being applied to someone (the Journeyman) who has demonstrated high skill.
variants.reason: A person with high skill on a task does not need step-by-step direction, but may still need supportive behavior to build confidence or ownership.

feedback.correct: Both statements are true, and the reason explains the assertion. The Journeyman has the competence to perform the task well, which is precisely why low directive behavior is appropriate — they don't need to be told how. But their dedication may be uneven (perhaps lacking confidence or full buy-in), so high supportive behavior addresses this gap. The strategy is designed around the specific contour of the Journeyman's needs.
feedback.incorrect: Consider the Journeyman's profile: high skill but sometimes variable dedication or confidence. They know how to do the task, making high directive behavior unnecessary and potentially patronizing. But they benefit from encouragement, listening, and shared decision-making — which is exactly what the Mentoring strategy's high supportive behavior provides.

questions.stem: In the DVULI version of the SMART goal framework used alongside the LSA, what does the 'M' in SMART stand for?
options: Motivating
Measurable
Meaningful
Manageable

feedback.correct: In the DVULI framework, SMART stands for Specific, Motivating, Attainable, Relevant, and Time-Bound. The use of 'Motivating' rather than the more common 'Measurable' reflects the DVULI emphasis on servant leadership — goals should not only be trackable but should inspire genuine engagement and dedication in the people being led.
feedback.incorrect: Many students default to 'Measurable' because it is the most widely known version of the SMART acronym in business contexts. However, the DVULI framework intentionally uses 'Motivating' to emphasize that effective goals in ministry leadership must energize the people pursuing them, not merely quantify outcomes.

variants.stem: A ministry leader sets this goal for a volunteer: 'Do better with the youth group this quarter.' According to the DVULI SMART framework (Specific, Motivating, Attainable, Relevant, Time-Bound), which criterion does this goal MOST clearly violate?
options: Specific — 'do better' is vague and provides no clear target or measurable outcome
Time-Bound — 'this quarter' is too long a timeframe for goal setting
Attainable — the goal is too ambitious for one volunteer to achieve
Relevant — youth group improvement is not a relevant ministry goal

feedback.correct: 'Do better' is the textbook example of a non-specific goal. It provides no clarity about what success looks like, what actions to take, or how progress would be recognized. Notice that 'this quarter' actually does provide a time frame, so Time-Bound is partially met — but the goal fundamentally fails on Specificity.
feedback.incorrect: While multiple SMART criteria can be applied, the most glaring failure is Specificity. 'Do better' gives the volunteer nothing concrete to work toward. The goal actually does include a time frame ('this quarter'), and youth group improvement is certainly relevant. The core problem is the absence of specific, defined outcomes.

questions.stem: According to the DISC framework from Session 2, what is the key difference between a person's Natural Style and their Adapted Style?
options: Natural Style reflects how you behave instinctively, while Adapted Style reflects how you adjust your behavior in response to your current environment or role
Natural Style is your behavior at work, while Adapted Style is your behavior at home
Natural Style is fixed at birth, while Adapted Style is the style you choose to develop through training
Natural Style applies to leadership situations, while Adapted Style applies to follower situations

feedback.correct: Your Natural Style is your default behavioral wiring — how you act when you're not thinking about how you're acting. Your Adapted Style reflects the adjustments you make in response to the demands of a specific context (such as a job, role, or relationship). A large gap between the two can signal stress or a poor environmental fit.
feedback.incorrect: The Natural/Adapted distinction is not about work versus home, or leadership versus followership. It's about your instinctive behavioral tendencies (Natural) versus how you modify those tendencies in response to situational demands (Adapted). Both styles operate in every context — the question is how much adjustment you're making.

variants.stem: In the DISC model from Session 2, a leader discovers a large gap between their Natural Style (high Steadiness) and their Adapted Style (high Dominance). What does this gap most likely indicate?
options: The leader's current environment is requiring them to behave in ways that differ significantly from their natural tendencies, which may create stress
The leader has successfully matured beyond their original personality type
The DISC assessment was scored incorrectly and should be retaken
The leader has a balanced personality that draws equally from all four styles

feedback.correct: A large Natural-to-Adapted gap means the person is significantly modifying their instinctive behavior to meet environmental demands. A naturally Steady person acting with high Dominance is pushing against their wiring, which can be effective short-term but often produces fatigue and stress if sustained without awareness.
feedback.incorrect: A large gap between Natural and Adapted styles does not mean the person has outgrown their personality or that the assessment is flawed. It signals significant behavioral adaptation — the person is acting quite differently from their natural tendencies, often because their role demands it. This awareness is valuable, not problematic.

questions.stem: Evaluate the relationship between these two statements:
questions.assertion: The Personal Assessment Model from Session 1 states that Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change produce Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability.
questions.reason: The three methods of self-discovery — reflecting on personal experience, feedback from others, and formal evaluation — are the same as the three outputs of the Personal Assessment Model.

feedback.correct: The assertion is accurate — the Personal Assessment Model does link Calling, Character, and Commitment to Change to the outcomes of Authenticity, Responsiveness, and Teachability. However, the reason is false. The three methods of self-discovery (personal reflection, feedback from others, formal evaluation) are METHODS for gaining self-knowledge, not the same as the three OUTCOMES of the Personal Assessment Model. These are two distinct frameworks from Session 1.
feedback.incorrect: Carefully distinguish between the Personal Assessment Model's outputs (Authenticity, Responsiveness, Teachability) and the three methods of self-discovery (reflection, feedback, formal evaluation). They are related concepts from the same session but are NOT the same thing. The model describes what mature self-knowledge produces; the methods describe how you gain self-knowledge.

variants.assertion: In Session 1, one of the three methods of self-discovery is receiving feedback from others, which connects to Paul's teaching about bearing one another's burdens in community.
variants.reason: The biblical basis for self-assessment includes the idea that honest self-knowledge is strengthened through relationships, not achieved in isolation.

feedback.correct: Both are true, and the reason explains the assertion. Feedback from others is indeed one of the three self-discovery methods, and it is grounded in the biblical principle that authentic self-knowledge requires community. Paul's teaching about burden-bearing and sober judgment assumes that we need others to see what we cannot see in ourselves.
feedback.incorrect: Both statements are actually accurate and logically connected. Feedback from others IS one of the three methods, and the biblical basis for self-assessment DOES emphasize that honest self-knowledge requires community input. The reason explains why feedback from others is theologically grounded as a method of self-discovery.

questions.stem: In Session 2, the DISC model uses biblical characters to illustrate how God works through different behavioral styles. Which of the following best reflects the session's teaching about behavioral diversity?
options: God uses all four behavioral styles for His purposes, and no single style is spiritually superior to the others
The most effective ministry leaders always score highest in the Influencing style because it relates to evangelism
Biblical leaders were effective because they never adapted their Natural Style
Steadiness and Conscientiousness are more Christ-like than Dominance and Influencing

feedback.correct: A central teaching of the DISC session is that God works through behavioral diversity. Biblical characters exemplify all four styles — none is spiritually superior. Paul's Dominance, Peter's Influencing, Abraham's Steadiness, and Moses's Conscientiousness were all used powerfully. The goal is self-awareness, not style change.
feedback.incorrect: The DISC framework does not rank styles as more or less spiritual. The session explicitly uses biblical exemplars from all four styles to demonstrate that God works through each. Believing one style is superior is itself a sign of limited self-awareness about behavioral diversity.

variants.stem: From Session 2, which statement best describes the purpose of studying your DISC profile in the context of ministry leadership?
options: To gain honest awareness of your behavioral tendencies so you can adapt more effectively to different people and situations
To identify which ministry role you are permanently best suited for based on your dominant trait
To determine whether your personality is compatible with leadership before committing to ministry
To change your Natural Style to match the most effective leadership profile

feedback.correct: The DISC session is fundamentally about self-awareness as a tool for effectiveness, not about labeling or limiting yourself. Understanding your Natural and Adapted styles helps you recognize your defaults, appreciate others' styles, and intentionally adjust your approach. The goal is adaptation, not personality change.
feedback.incorrect: DISC is not about finding your permanent role or changing who you are. It's a self-awareness tool that helps you understand your behavioral tendencies so you can make informed adjustments. Session 2 taught adaptation strategies — adjusting your approach — not personality replacement.

questions.stem: Classify each statement about the LSA framework as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: A person's development step is the same across every task they perform.
The Instructing strategy involves the leader making most of the decisions about how the task is done.
A highly experienced ministry leader can be at the Novice development step.
The Commissioning strategy means the leader has completely abandoned the follower.
The Mentoring strategy is appropriate when a person has high skill but variable dedication.
Using the same leadership strategy for every person on your team produces optimal results.
A person who was once a Master on a task may regress to a lower development step.

feedback.correct: The key insight is that development steps are task-specific and dynamic, not permanent labels. A veteran leader can be a Novice at a new task. A former Master can regress if context changes. And strategies must always be matched — no single approach fits everyone. Commissioning is intentional delegation, not abandonment.
feedback.incorrect: The most common errors involve treating development steps as fixed, global traits rather than task-specific, situational assessments. Remember: development step is determined by Skill and Dedication on a SPECIFIC task at a SPECIFIC time. Also, Commissioning is a deliberate strategy of entrusting autonomy to a proven Master — it still involves periodic check-ins and is not abandonment.

variants.stem: Classify each statement about LSA leadership strategies and development steps as 'Always true,' 'Sometimes true,' or 'Never true':

statements.text: High directive behavior means the leader is being controlling or authoritarian.
The Developing strategy includes significant encouragement alongside clear direction.
A Journeyman who gains full confidence and dedication on a task should be moved to Commissioning.
A leader's most-used LSA strategy is always the correct one for their context.
Over-instructing a Master can lead to frustration and disengagement.
A Novice on a task is also a Novice on every other task.
A team member can move from Novice to Master without passing through every intermediate step.

feedback.correct: High directive behavior is not inherently negative — for Novices, it is exactly what servant leadership looks like. The key is matching. Over-instructing a Master feels like micromanagement, but the same behavior directed at a Novice feels like much-needed guidance. And development steps are task-specific — being a Novice at one task says nothing about your level on another.
feedback.incorrect: A frequent misconception is that directive behavior is inherently un-servant-like. In the LSA framework, providing clear direction to someone who needs it IS serving them. Similarly, many assume development is always linear, but people can sometimes accelerate rapidly on a task or regress when circumstances change.

questions.stem: Imagine a ministry team of 8 people, with 2 members at each development step (2 Novices, 2 Apprentices, 2 Journeymen, 2 Masters). If their leader defaults to using ONLY the Instructing strategy for everyone, approximately what percentage of the team is receiving a MISMATCHED leadership approach?

feedback.correct: The answer is 75%. Instructing (high directive, low supportive) correctly matches only the Novice step. The 2 Apprentices, 2 Journeymen, and 2 Masters — 6 out of 8 people, or 75% — are all receiving the wrong strategy. This illustrates why defaulting to a single strategy is so costly: it guarantees mismatch for most of your team.
feedback.incorrect: Many people underestimate the mismatch rate because they think Instructing is 'safe' or 'close enough' for most people. But Instructing only matches one of the four development steps (Novice). If each step is equally represented, three-quarters of the team — 75% — is being led with the wrong strategy, producing frustration, micromanagement, and stifled growth.

variants.stem: In the LSA model, two of the four leadership strategies involve high supportive behavior (Developing and Mentoring), and two involve low supportive behavior (Instructing and Commissioning). What percentage of the four LSA strategies include HIGH DIRECTIVE behavior as a component?

feedback.correct: The answer is 50%. Two of the four strategies — Instructing (high directive, low supportive) and Developing (high directive, high supportive) — include high directive behavior. The other two — Mentoring and Commissioning — are both low directive. This means half the strategies emphasize structure and direction, and half emphasize follower autonomy.
feedback.incorrect: Count carefully: Instructing is high directive, Developing is high directive, Mentoring is LOW directive, and Commissioning is LOW directive. That's 2 out of 4, or 50%. A common mistake is confusing Developing with a low-directive strategy because of its supportive component, but Developing is high in BOTH dimensions.

questions.stem: Pastor Diane leads a youth ministry team. She sets this goal for everyone: 'Let's improve our outreach this year.' She then assigns Keisha — a brand-new volunteer with no prior ministry experience — to independently design and launch a new neighborhood outreach program, telling her, 'I trust you completely; do it however you think is best.' Meanwhile, she gives James — a five-year veteran who has successfully created and led three outreach programs — a detailed daily task checklist and personally reviews every conversation he has with families. Using the full LSA framework, which conclusion BEST captures the problems in Pastor Diane's approach?
options: Diane has committed two strategy-step mismatches — over-commissioning a Novice and over-instructing a Master — compounded by a team goal that fails SMART criteria
Diane's only real problem is the vague goal; if she made it SMART, both Keisha and James would perform well regardless of strategy
Diane should use the Developing strategy for both people since it provides the best balance of direction and support for any situation
Diane's real issue is that she hasn't assessed Keisha's and James's DISC profiles, which would tell her exactly which strategy to use
justifications: Keisha is a Novice who needs Instructing (high directive), not Commissioning; James is a Master who needs Commissioning (low directive), not Instructing; and 'improve our outreach this year' fails Specific, Motivating, and Attainable SMART criteria — all three errors undermine effectiveness
The strategy mismatches are problematic but secondary — SMART goal setting is the foundational LSA skill, so fixing the goal alone would resolve most of the performance issues
Developing is the safest default strategy because it is high in both directive and supportive behavior, meaning it partially meets everyone's needs regardless of their development step
DISC profiles directly determine which LSA strategy should be used — a Dominant person needs Commissioning while a Steady person needs Instructing — so knowing styles would fix the mismatch

feedback.correct: This scenario weaves together three core LSA concepts: development step analysis, strategy matching, and SMART goal setting. Keisha (Novice) needs the structure of Instructing, not the autonomy of Commissioning. James (Master) needs the trust of Commissioning, not the oversight of Instructing. And 'improve our outreach this year' fails multiple SMART criteria — it's not Specific (what counts as improvement?), may not be Motivating (it's generic), and lacks clear Attainability. Diane has essentially inverted her leadership and built it on a fuzzy goal. The 2 Timothy 2:2 principle of developing self-reliance requires correctly diagnosing what each person needs and setting clear goals for them to pursue.
feedback.incorrect: The most common error is reducing the problem to a single dimension. Some students see only the goal problem, thinking SMART goals alone fix everything. Others see only the strategy mismatch. But the LSA framework integrates all three skills: goal setting, analyzing the development step, and matching the strategy. Diane has failed on all three, and each failure compounds the others.
feedback.wrongReason: Students who correctly identify the dual mismatch and goal failure sometimes select a justification that elevates SMART goals above strategy matching, or that treats Developing as a universal solution. The correct reasoning recognizes that all three LSA skills operate together — no single fix is sufficient. DISC style does NOT determine LSA strategy; the follower's development step does.

variants.stem: Marcus oversees a church's children's ministry team. His annual goal for the team is: 'Be excellent.' He has two key volunteers: Tanya, who has led children's worship for six years and consistently receives glowing parent feedback, and Devon, who joined last month and has never worked with children before. Marcus decides to shadow Devon at every session, script his exact words, and correct him immediately when he deviates — but also offers no encouragement, no explanation for why tasks are done a certain way, and no check-ins on how Devon is feeling about the role. For Tanya, Marcus requires her to submit weekly lesson plans for his approval and attend a mandatory basics-of-teaching workshop. Using the LSA framework, which statement BEST identifies the core problems?
options: Marcus is close to the right strategy for Devon (Instructing) but is missing the encouragement dimension at the right time; he is over-instructing Tanya (a Master) who should be Commissioned; and 'Be excellent' fails as a SMART goal
Marcus is correctly using Instructing for Devon and should simply add the same level of structure for Tanya to maintain fairness
Marcus needs to switch to Mentoring for both volunteers since ministry leadership should always prioritize supportive behavior over directive behavior
The only problem is the goal — if Marcus defined 'excellent' using SMART criteria, his strategies for both volunteers would be effective
justifications: Devon as a Novice needs Instructing's high directive behavior, which Marcus partially provides, but Novices also benefit from task-focused direction without punitive correction; Tanya as a likely Master needs Commissioning's autonomy, not basic workshops; and 'Be excellent' fails Specific, Attainable, and Time-Bound SMART criteria
The main issue is Marcus's DISC style — he is likely high Dominance, which makes him naturally directive with everyone, and style awareness alone would fix the problem
Mentoring (low directive, high supportive) is always the most Christ-like strategy because it prioritizes relationship over task, which should be the default in ministry
Marcus correctly identified that both volunteers need high directive leadership because ministry with children requires extra structure regardless of volunteer experience

feedback.correct: This scenario tests nuanced understanding. Marcus is partially right with Devon — Novices do need high directive behavior — but effective Instructing is structured and educational, not punitive. Tanya is clearly a Master being treated like a Novice, which the LSA framework predicts will produce frustration and disengagement. And 'Be excellent' is the opposite of a SMART goal. All three LSA leader skills — goal setting, analyzing, and matching — are needed simultaneously.
