feedback.incorrect: Be careful not to collapse the problem into a single cause. While Marcus's personality might contribute, DISC style does not determine which LSA strategy is appropriate — the follower's development step does. And Mentoring is not universally best; it matches only the Journeyman step. Each person needs the strategy that fits their specific skill and dedication profile.
feedback.wrongReason: Students who see the strategy mismatches and goal failure sometimes choose a justification that blames Marcus's DISC style or that elevates one strategy as universally best. The LSA framework explicitly teaches that there is no one best strategy — the right strategy is always the one that matches the person's development step on the specific task.

prompts.prompt: Which is worse: a boss who micromanages every little thing you do, or a boss who gives you zero guidance and just disappears? Which one have you actually experienced?
True or false: The best way to learn something new is to be thrown in the deep end with no help and figure it out yourself. Do you actually believe that, or does it depend?
Think about the best leader, coach, or teacher you've ever had. Did they give you a lot of freedom — or did they keep you on a pretty tight leash? What made them so good?
Have you ever been really good at something and then had someone treat you like a total beginner? How did that feel — annoying, insulting, fine? Where's the line?
Do people who write down specific goals actually do better than people who just 'try their best'? Or is that motivational poster nonsense?
Would you rather be mentored by someone who's incredibly skilled but terrible at explaining things, or someone who's average at the job but an amazing teacher?

activities.title: Skill + Dedication Matrix: Sort the Person
activities.description: The facilitator shares a Jamboard or Miro board pre-loaded with a 2x2 grid (Skill on one axis, Dedication on the other) and four sticky-note character cards — one for each development step (Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, Master). Each character card includes a brief narrative description of a real employee situation (e.g., 'Tanya just transferred into the role, is highly motivated, but has never done this type of work before'). Students are placed into four breakout rooms of ~6 people and given 5 minutes to place all four cards in the correct quadrant AND label which development step each person represents. Rooms rejoin the main session and a spokesperson for each group shares their placements using screen share or the shared board. The facilitator leads a 3-minute debrief highlighting any disagreements across groups, especially cases where Dedication is low — a common source of confusion. The goal is for students to practice the analytical diagnosis step before strategy selection.
activities.materials: Jamboard, Miro, or shared Google Slide with pre-built 2x2 grid and character scenario cards; Zoom breakout room capability
activities.learningGoal: Reinforces the Skill + Dedication = Development Step framework and the ability to accurately analyze where a person sits before choosing a strategy
activities.scalingNotes: For groups under 15, run as one shared discussion rather than breakouts. For groups over 40, add more character cards and use 5-6 breakout rooms; have groups post their answers in the chat simultaneously for a quick visual comparison before debrief.

activities.title: Strategy Showdown: Defend Your Match
activities.description: The facilitator posts a single, deliberately ambiguous scenario in the chat — a person who appears to be between development steps (e.g., high skill, inconsistent dedication). A poll is launched immediately asking each student to vote for which of the four LSA strategies — Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, or Commissioning — they would apply. After results are revealed (expected to be split), the facilitator assigns students with different votes to mixed breakout rooms of 4-5 people and gives them 6 minutes to build a shared case for a single strategy — they must reach consensus or at minimum identify the strongest argument. Groups return and one spokesperson per room presents their chosen strategy and the reasoning in 60 seconds. The facilitator then revisits the diagnostic framework: what development step did each group assign the person, and did that drive their strategy choice? This surfaces the insight that mismatched strategies usually start with a misdiagnosed development step.
activities.materials: Zoom poll (or Mentimeter), Zoom breakout rooms, scenario displayed in chat or shared screen
activities.learningGoal: Practices the full LSA leader skill sequence — Analyzing the development step and Matching the strategy — and builds tolerance for ambiguity in real-world situations
activities.scalingNotes: Works best with 16-30 students. For smaller groups (under 15), skip breakouts and run as a live whole-group debate in chat + verbal discussion. For larger groups, run two parallel scenarios to keep energy up and compare results across both at the end.

activities.title: SMART Goal Makeover Workshop
activities.description: The facilitator shares a Google Doc or collaborative slide deck containing three deliberately weak development goals (e.g., 'Help Marcus get better at communication' or 'Improve Sarah's confidence in the role'). Student pairs are assigned via breakout rooms and given 6 minutes to rewrite all three goals using the SMART framework, making them specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and time-bound. Each pair pastes their rewritten goals into a shared response column in the doc so all versions are visible when the class reconvenes. The facilitator then does a rapid 'gallery walk' using screen share, reading two or three versions of the same goal and asking the group to vote via reaction emoji (thumbs up / thumbs down) on whether each is truly SMART. The session closes by identifying the most common SMART element that students left vague — typically 'Measurable' — and drilling into why that element matters most in a development context.
activities.materials: Shared Google Doc or Google Slides with weak goal examples and a response column; Zoom breakout rooms
activities.learningGoal: Builds the Goal Setting (SMART) leader skill and connects well-formed goals to effective use of the LSA development step
activities.scalingNotes: For cohorts over 30, assign goals so each pair only rewrites one goal rather than all three — this keeps sharing time manageable. For smaller groups, do this as a whole class using live annotation on a shared screen and collaborative editing in real time.

activities.title: Dial It Up / Dial It Down: Directive vs. Supportive
activities.description: The facilitator opens a Mentimeter (or Google Form) with a visual 'slider' scale from 'High Directive / Low Supportive' to 'Low Directive / High Supportive' and presents each of the four LSA strategies one at a time. Students submit where they believe each strategy sits on that behavioral spectrum. After all four are submitted, the facilitator reveals the aggregated results and displays the intended answer alongside, highlighting any strategies the group misplaced — Developing is the most commonly confused because it is high on both dimensions simultaneously. The facilitator then asks students to explain in the chat what a 'high directive AND high supportive' behavior actually looks like in a real conversation, prompting 3-4 students to share examples. The debrief ties each strategy's behavioral profile back to the development step it serves, reinforcing why the match matters.
activities.materials: Mentimeter slider activity (or Google Form with scale questions) configured before class; no breakout rooms required
activities.learningGoal: Clarifies how directive and supportive behaviors combine differently across the four strategies and why those combinations align with specific development steps
activities.scalingNotes: This activity is size-agnostic and works equally well for 10 or 100 students since it uses asynchronous polling. For larger groups, use the chat reaction to surface examples rather than calling on individuals verbally to avoid bottlenecks.

activities.title: Leader Hot Seat: Diagnose and Decide in Real Time
activities.description:
You are presented with three ministry volunteer profiles, one at a time. Each profile describes a real task, the volunteer's observable behavior on that task, and two or three details that point toward a specific development step — but the step is not named for you. Read each profile, identify the development step you diagnose, then select the LSA leadership strategy you would apply and type a one-sentence rationale.

After you submit each response, the correct development step and matching strategy are revealed with a brief explanation of which clues in the profile were the diagnostic signals and why the other strategies would misfire at that step. If your strategy selection was wrong, the feedback identifies the specific quadrant mismatch — for example, whether you over-supported a high-competence volunteer who needed autonomy, or under-directed someone still building the skill.

Work through all three profiles before moving on. The goal is not just to name the quadrant but to practice reading behavioral evidence the way you will need to in a real conversation — under the slight pressure of committing to a call before you see the answer.
activities.materials: Zoom chat or breakout rooms for scenario writing; no additional tools required
activities.learningGoal: Integrates all three LSA leader skills — Goal Setting orientation, Analyzing the development step, and Matching the strategy — in a fast-paced, socially dynamic format
activities.scalingNotes: For groups under 15, skip the breakout rooms and write scenarios individually or in pairs in the chat. For groups over 35, run two simultaneous hot seat sessions in separate breakout rooms with a facilitator or confident participant moderating each one, then debrief together in the main room.

activities.title: The Strategy Mismatch Museum
activities.description: Before the activity, the facilitator prepares a shared slide deck with five slides — each showing a short scenario in which a leader uses the wrong LSA strategy for a person's development step (e.g., Commissioning a Novice, or Instructing a Master). Students are given access to the deck and 5 minutes to add a sticky note or text comment to each slide identifying: (1) what development step the person actually is, (2) what strategy the leader wrongly chose, and (3) what the likely consequence of that mismatch is for the person's development. The class then reconvenes, and the facilitator walks through each 'exhibit' in the museum, reading out two or three responses per slide, focusing on the consequence — this is where the richest conversation emerges. The debrief emphasizes that every strategy is correct somewhere and damaging somewhere else, which crystallizes the 'right fit' concept from the course title itself.
activities.materials: Shared Google Slides deck with 5 pre-built mismatch scenario slides; students need editing or commenting access; no breakout rooms required
activities.learningGoal: Deepens understanding of why strategy-to-development-step matching matters by examining the real costs of mismatches, reinforcing all four strategies and all four development steps through contrast and consequence
activities.scalingNotes: Works well for any size between 10 and 50 students. For larger groups, assign each student only one or two slides to comment on rather than all five to avoid overwhelming the debrief. For very small groups (under 12), have students discuss their answers verbally rather than asynchronously annotating the deck.

slides.title: The Art of the Right Fit
slides.bodyText: What if the wrong leadership style — even a great one — is worse than no leadership at all?
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome everyone. This chapter is about one of the most practical skills a leader can develop: not just knowing what to do, but knowing what to do *for this person, right now*. We're going to build a full toolkit today — a way of reading people, diagnosing situations, and choosing the exact right strategy. By the end, you'll have four strategies, a four-level framework for understanding where people are, and three skills to tie it all together. Let's get into it.

slides.title: Where Are They Right Now?
slides.bodyText: Before you can lead effectively, you have to see your people clearly — their skill, their dedication, and where those two intersect.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition into the first major section. Ask students: 'Has anyone ever been micromanaged on something you were already great at? Or been left alone on something you had no idea how to do?' Both feel wrong — and they are wrong. The first section is about diagnosing exactly where someone is so you never make either mistake.

slides.title: The Two Dimensions of Every Follower
bullets: Skill — knowledge, experience, demonstrated ability
Dedication — motivation, confidence, commitment to the goal
Neither alone tells the full story
Both must be assessed for the specific task — not in general
slides.speakerNotes: Stress that this is task-specific, not person-specific. A surgeon might be a Master in the OR but a Novice learning a new surgical robot. Don't fall into the trap of labeling people globally — always anchor the assessment to the specific goal you set together. Ask: 'What's an example where your skill and dedication were mismatched for a task?'

slides.title: The Development Step Framework
bullets: Novice — low skill, high dedication (eager but new)
Apprentice — some skill, lower dedication (the slump)
Journeyman — solid skill, variable dedication (almost there)
Master — high skill, high dedication (self-directed)
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each level with a vivid example. The Novice is the enthusiastic new hire — lots of energy, not much know-how yet. The Apprentice hits a wall — they know enough to see how hard this is, and motivation dips. This is the most dangerous step to misread. The Journeyman is competent but may need a confidence push. The Master is ready to fly solo. Ask students: 'Which step is the easiest to misdiagnose — and why?' The Apprentice usually wins this vote.

slides.title: The Core Insight
slides.bodyText: Skill and Dedication don't move together — and the gap between them is exactly where leaders earn their keep.
slides.speakerNotes: Pause here. Let this land. The trap most leaders fall into is assuming that as skill grows, motivation automatically follows. It doesn't. The Apprentice stage is proof: someone can get more capable and less motivated at the same time. Write this on the board if you can. This is the idea students should photograph. Ask: 'When have you seen someone's dedication drop even as they got better at something?'

slides.title: Directive vs. Supportive: The Building Blocks
bullets: Directive — tells, shows, structures, corrects
High directive: close supervision, clear instructions
Low directive: hands off, follower drives the task
slides.bodyText: ["Supportive — listens, encourages, facilitates, affirms", "High supportive: emotional investment, two-way dialogue", "Low supportive: minimal relationship maintenance needed"]
slides.speakerNotes: This is the engine under the hood of all four strategies. Every strategy is just a different combination of these two dials. Walk through the quadrants concept — high D / low S, high D / high S, low D / high S, low D / low S. Ask students to predict which combination matches which development step before you reveal the strategies. It creates buy-in and tests their intuition.

slides.title: The Four Strategies Await
slides.bodyText: Each strategy is a precise prescription — the right blend of direction and support for exactly one development step.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the pivot point of the chapter. We've built the diagnosis tool — now we build the response toolkit. Remind students: knowing the four strategies without being able to diagnose the development step is useless. And diagnosing perfectly but picking the wrong strategy is just as bad. The power is in the match. Let's meet the four strategies.

slides.title: Instructing & Developing
bullets: Instructing → Novice: high directive, low supportive
Leader structures, explains, demonstrates step-by-step
Enthusiasm is there — skill is not yet; don't squash the energy
Developing → Apprentice: high directive, high supportive
Leader coaches AND encourages; skill-building + morale repair
slides.speakerNotes: The Instructing strategy is NOT harsh — it's clear. The Novice doesn't need cheerleading yet; they need a roadmap. But you still acknowledge their enthusiasm. Developing is the most demanding strategy for the leader — you're simultaneously building skill (high directive) AND rebuilding confidence (high supportive). Give the example of a new employee who just hit their first major failure at month 3. That's a Developing moment. Ask: 'What's the risk if you use Instructing on an Apprentice?'

slides.title: Mentoring & Commissioning
bullets: Mentoring → Journeyman: low directive, high supportive
Competence is there — leader facilitates, asks, affirms
Goal: build confidence to match capability
Commissioning → Master: low directive, low supportive
Full autonomy; leader steps back and trusts completely
slides.speakerNotes: Mentoring is often misunderstood — leaders think it means giving lots of advice. Actually, in the LSA model, Mentoring means mostly listening and affirming. The Journeyman knows what to do; they just need someone to believe in them. Commissioning is the hardest strategy for controlling leaders — letting go feels like abandonment, but for a Master it's the ultimate sign of respect. Ask: 'What happens to a Master who gets Instructed? Why does that backfire?'

slides.title: Ken Blanchard
slides.bodyText: "Different strokes for different folks — and different strokes for the same folks at different times."
slides.speakerNotes: Blanchard's situational leadership work underpins much of the LSA model. The second half of this quote is the sleeper insight — it's not just about knowing your people, it's about knowing your people *right now*, on *this task*. People move through development steps, regress under stress, and surprise you. The leader who checks in only once has already fallen behind. Ask: 'When have you changed development steps on the same task over time?'

slides.title: The Three Skills That Make It Work
slides.bodyText: Knowing the strategies is table stakes. The three leader skills are what turn knowledge into execution.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition into the final major section. Tell students: 'You now have the diagnosis tool and the strategy menu. This section is about the three skills that tie everything together — how you actually do this in real time with real people.' These skills are Goal Setting, Analyzing, and Matching — in that exact order, because sequence matters.

slides.title: Skill 1: Goal Setting with SMART
bullets: Specific — no ambiguity about what success looks like
Measurable — progress must be observable and trackable
Attainable — stretch without breaking confidence
Relevant — connected to the person's role and growth
Time-bound — a deadline creates accountability
slides.speakerNotes: Goals come first — always. You cannot analyze a development step in the abstract. You assess skill and dedication *relative to a specific goal*. Walk through a bad goal vs. a SMART goal: 'Do better on presentations' vs. 'Deliver a 10-minute project brief to the leadership team by Friday with no more than two follow-up clarifying questions.' Ask students to rewrite a vague goal in SMART format — quick 2-minute pair exercise works well here.

slides.title: Skills 2 & 3: Analyze, Then Match
bullets: Analyzing — assess skill AND dedication for this goal
Avoid the halo effect: past performance ≠ present step
Ask, observe, and check your assumptions
Matching — select the strategy that fits the step
Mismatch = frustration, stagnation, or learned helplessness
slides.speakerNotes: The halo effect is the biggest threat to accurate analysis — leaders see a high performer and assume Master in everything. Stress that analysis is ongoing, not a one-time event. On Matching: have students map it out — Novice → Instructing, Apprentice → Developing, Journeyman → Mentoring, Master → Commissioning. Then throw a curveball: 'What if your Master takes on a brand-new responsibility? What step are they on now?' Watch them recalibrate in real time.

slides.title: Strategy to Development Step
bullets: Novice → Instructing (High D / Low S)
Apprentice → Developing (High D / High S)
Journeyman → Mentoring (Low D / High S)
slides.bodyText: ["Clear structure before cheerleading", "Skill-building AND confidence repair", "Affirm capability; let them lead", "Full autonomy; trust as the reward"]
slides.speakerNotes: This is the master reference slide — the full map in one view. Left column is the diagnosis; right column is the philosophy behind each strategy. Walk through all four rows together. Then ask: 'Which mismatch would be most damaging — Commissioning a Novice, or Instructing a Master?' Both are bad, but for very different reasons. Novice without structure flounders; Master under micromanagement disengages or leaves.

slides.title: The Right Fit Is a Moving Target
slides.bodyText: Leadership mastery isn't picking a style and sticking with it — it's staying curious about where your people are today.
slides.speakerNotes: This is the emotional close of the chapter. The best leaders aren't the ones with the most authority or the loudest presence — they're the ones who keep asking: 'Where is this person right now, on this goal, today?' That question is a habit of mind. Let this sit. Ask students to name one person they lead or will lead, and privately identify what development step they're on for a specific goal. That's their homework starting point.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: Development Step = Skill + Dedication on a specific goal (Novice → Master)
Four strategies map to four steps: Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, Commissioning
Directive and supportive behaviors combine differently in each strategy
Three leader skills — Goal Setting, Analyzing, Matching — must happen in sequence
The mismatch, not the difficulty, is what derails people
slides.speakerNotes: Summarize the full arc: we started with a question about fit, built a diagnostic framework (development steps), introduced four targeted strategies, unpacked the directive/supportive engine behind them, and finished with the three skills that make it all actionable. Ask students to write down one insight they'll apply this week. If time allows: quick round-robin share. Preview next session — we'll put this model under real pressure with case scenarios.

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
Picture two youth workers you lead. The first is James — he arrived three weeks ago from a background in corporate training, brimming with energy and ideas, eager to run his first mid-week Bible study. He has never done it before, but his dedication is electric. The second is Maria — she has facilitated your outreach events for nine years, knows every family in the neighborhood by name, and has navigated every program challenge imaginable. But lately she comes in late, seems distracted, and leaves early. Something has shifted in her motivation since the pastoral transition eight months ago.

Now here is the question that determines whether your team thrives or fractures: Do you lead James and Maria the same way? If your instinct is to roll out a detailed orientation manual for both of them, or conversely to simply tell both "you have got this — go for it," you are doing what most ministry leaders do by default. And default leadership, as we will see in this session, is one of the most quietly costly mistakes in the kingdom enterprise.

[short pause]

There is no single best leadership strategy. This is the deceptively radical claim at the heart of the LEADERSHIP STRATEGY ASSESSMENT framework — referred to throughout this session as the L-S-A — used throughout the DeVos Urban Leadership Initiative, which we will call DVULI. This claim runs counter to much of the leadership literature we have absorbed over the years — literature that tends to crown one particular style as the gold standard. The L-S-A, grounded in situational leadership theory as Hersey, Blanchard, and Johnson described in 2008, insists that leadership effectiveness is never about the leader's preferred style. It is always about the match between a leader's approach and the DEVELOPMENT LEVEL of the specific person on the specific task at hand.

That word specific carries a great deal of weight. Development level is not a personality trait or a career ranking. It is task-specific. Maria, your nine-year veteran, might be a Master at facilitating neighborhood outreach events — but if you ask her to build and manage a new social media strategy she has never touched, she may well be a Novice. James may be a complete Novice at Bible study facilitation but a Journeyman at building donor relationships because of his corporate background. The L-S-A framework insists that leaders resist the powerful temptation to categorize people globally and instead analyze carefully for each task — as Blanchard, Zigarmi, and Zigarmi emphasized in 2013.

Research on situational leadership has consistently affirmed this task-specific logic. Graeff's extensive 1997 review of the theoretical and empirical literature on situational leadership concluded that task maturity — a follower's readiness level for a specific assignment — is the primary driver of which leadership approach produces superior outcomes. The core insight is not complicated, but its application requires ongoing attentiveness: great leaders shift their strategy to fit the person and the task, not the other way around.

[short pause]

Before moving further, bring to mind one person you currently lead. Think of a specific task you have assigned them. On a gut level, how skilled and how motivated do you sense they are for that task? Now ask yourself honestly: does the way you are leading them on that task reflect a genuine assessment, or does it mostly reflect how you tend to lead everyone?

[short pause]

The DVULI workbook identifies three interlocking skills that define an effective L-S-A leader. These are not philosophical virtues but practical competencies — things you can learn, practice, and improve. They build on one another sequentially: you cannot match a strategy well if you have not analyzed the development step, and you cannot analyze accurately if you have not set a proper goal in the first place.

The first skill is GOAL SETTING — coordinating what needs to be accomplished and when for those being led. The second is Analyzing — working with those being led to determine their skill and dedication level for a specific task they are trying to accomplish. The third is Matching — properly applying various leadership strategies to equip those being led with what is needed for a specific task.

Notice that this sequence is deliberate and non-negotiable. Without a clearly articulated goal, there is no common reference point for analyzing where someone is in their development on that task. And without accurate analysis, any strategy you apply is essentially guesswork, however well-intentioned — as Bjerke and Renger demonstrated in 2017. The DVULI framework is explicit: without first setting proper goals, an L-S-A leader cannot most effectively lead others.

[short pause]

Every L-S-A leadership conversation begins with a goal — not a vague aspiration, but what the framework calls a SMART goal. SMART is an acronym standing for Specific, Motivating, Attainable, Relevant, and Time-Bound. Let us walk through each of these criteria as they apply in the DVULI context.

A Specific goal clearly answers what exactly the task is and what a good job looks like. A Motivating goal asks whether the person is genuinely excited and interested in this work. An Attainable goal ensures that the task is realistic and achievable given current resources and constraints. A Relevant goal confirms that the task is important and aligned with the organization's mission and goals. And a Time-Bound goal establishes a clear way to track progress, with defined measures and deadlines.

The empirical case for SMART goal-setting is formidable. Locke, Shaw, Saari, and Latham's landmark 1981 meta-analytic review — synthesizing over a decade of goal-setting research — established that specific, challenging goals consistently produce higher performance than vague or simply "do your best" goals, with goal clarity emerging as one of the three most critical moderators of performance outcomes. More recently, Bjerke and Renger in 2017 demonstrated that SMART goal quality — particularly specificity and attainability — significantly moderates the relationship between leader support and follower motivation. In simple terms: a poorly constructed goal undermines even excellent leadership support.

In the DVULI context, SMART goals serve a ministry-specific purpose that goes beyond performance management. They create the clarity of expectation that respects the dignity of those we lead. Vague expectations — "be more intentional about discipleship" or "take more initiative with volunteers" — place people in an impossible position. They cannot succeed at a target they cannot see. Writing a SMART goal is an act of servant love: it gives the person you lead the gift of knowing exactly what success looks like, so they can pursue it with confidence.

The M for Motivating in the DVULI SMART model deserves particular attention. Traditional SMART frameworks use Measurable, but DVULI's adaptation reframes this criterion around the follower's interior relationship to the task. Is this person genuinely energized by this work? This matters because motivation — what DVULI calls dedication — is one of the two core variables that determine a person's development step. A goal that fails to connect with what the person actually cares about is a goal that undermines the entire L-S-A process before it begins. The five SMART goal criteria work together as interlocking components — removing any one undermines the whole foundation of the L-S-A process.

[short pause]

Once a proper SMART goal is in place, the L-S-A leader's next task is to analyze the DEVELOPMENT STEP of the person being led — specifically in relation to that goal. Development step is determined by two variables operating together: Skill and Dedication.

Skill in the DVULI framework includes both proven task-specific knowledge and abilities — understanding what the task requires, having done it before, being able to show examples of the work, knowing how long it takes — and proven transferable knowledge and skills, such as planning, interpersonal communication, problem-solving, and project management capabilities that may apply from other contexts.

Dedication encompasses both motivation and confidence on a specific task. Motivation includes being energized, focused, positive, and passionate about the task. Confidence includes being self-assured, independent, self-reliant, and willing to initiate. This dual nature of dedication is crucial: a person can be highly motivated but deeply lacking in confidence — as is common with excited beginners — or highly confident but disengaged — as often occurs when a highly skilled veteran loses enthusiasm for familiar work.

Taken together, the DVULI framework identifies four development steps, each representing a different combination of skill and dedication for a particular task. And it is essential to emphasize what the workbook makes explicit: development step is task-specific. It is not a label for a person's general capacity or value — it is a diagnostic snapshot of where they are on one particular assignment at one particular moment. A Journeyman at preaching might be a Novice at financial reporting. A Master at one-on-one mentoring might be an Apprentice at leading large group events.

[short pause]

The four development steps, from least to most developed on a given task, are as follows.

The NOVICE is new to the task — enthusiastic, eager to learn, but confused about the work and inexperienced. The Novice shows high dedication combined with low skill.

The APPRENTICE is learning the task but showing inconsistent performance. They are often discouraged and frustrated, not always confident. The Apprentice has some developing skill but lower dedication than when they started.

The Journeyman is generally skillful and makes productive contributions, but is not always confident and may be self-assured only at times. The Journeyman shows higher skill paired with variable dedication.

The Master is accomplished, consistently competent, and self-reliant. They may at times be bored with the familiar work, and they are fully able to teach others. The Master demonstrates high skill combined with high dedication.

The progression from Novice to Master is not linear in experience alone — it is a journey through shifting combinations of skill and dedication. The most counterintuitive step in this sequence is the Apprentice. A person at the Apprentice step typically has more skill than a Novice but has lost some of their initial excitement. They have encountered the real difficulty of the task. The early optimism of the Novice has met reality, and the result is often discouragement or self-doubt. This disillusioned learner dynamic is well documented in the Situational Leadership II literature, as Blanchard, Zigarmi, and Zigarmi described in 2013: leaders who fail to recognize the Apprentice's drop in dedication often make the fatal error of backing away — thinking "they are learning, I will give them space" — precisely when the person most needs structured encouragement and continued direction.

The Journeyman presents a different diagnostic challenge. This is a person with genuinely developed skill — they can do the work, and do it well most of the time. But their dedication fluctuates. They may have the ability but lack the confidence to operate independently. Or they may have both ability and confidence but feel insufficiently engaged, unseen, or uncertain about the importance of their contribution. This is where Maria, our nine-year veteran, is likely operating: high skill, but motivation dimmed. The Journeyman does not need more instruction about how to do the task. What she often needs is a leader who asks her opinion, invests in her motivation, and helps her rediscover the meaning of the work she already knows how to do.

[short pause]

Think about the five people on your team or volunteer roster whom you spend the most time managing. For each of them, identify one key task in their role. What do you sense their development step is on that task? Are you more confident in diagnosing their skill level or their dedication level? What does that tell you about where your blind spots might be as an analyzer?

[short pause]

With a SMART goal established and the development step accurately diagnosed, the L-S-A leader is ready to select and apply the appropriate leadership strategy. The DVULI framework identifies four strategies, each defined by its unique combination of directive and supportive leadership behaviors.

Directive leadership behaviors involve the leader firmly overseeing task performance through a degree of one-way communication — outlining what the requirements of the task are, and how and when things should be accomplished. Seven specific directive behaviors include setting SMART goals, showing and telling how, establishing timelines, identifying priorities, clarifying roles, developing action plans, and monitoring and tracking performance.

SUPPORTIVE leadership behaviors involve the leader drawing the person more deeply into decision-making through two-way communication — developing mutual trust and respect, which results in increased motivation and confidence. Seven specific supportive behaviors include listening, facilitating self-reliant problem solving, asking for input, providing rationale, acknowledging and encouraging, sharing information about the organization, and sharing information about oneself.

The four L-S-A strategies combine these behavioral dimensions in different proportions, each calibrated to serve a specific development step.

[short pause]

The first strategy is INSTRUCTING: high directive, low support, designed for the Novice. The Novice — enthusiastic but inexperienced — needs structure, clarity, and clear direction above all else. The Instructing strategy delivers high directive behaviors — showing and telling how, establishing timelines, clarifying roles, monitoring performance — while keeping supportive behaviors relatively lower. This is not because the Novice does not need encouragement — they do — but because what primarily moves the Novice toward competence is clarity about what to do and how to do it. Excessive problem-solving dialogue with a Novice, before they have a foundation of knowledge, is often confusing and counterproductive. The Novice needs a leader who will walk alongside them closely, demonstrate competence, and provide a clear roadmap.

The second strategy is DEVELOPING: high directive, high support, designed for the Apprentice. The Apprentice still needs significant direction — they have not yet achieved consistent competence — but the central challenge has shifted from confusion to discouragement. The Developing strategy maintains high directive behaviors while dramatically increasing supportive behaviors: listening actively, acknowledging effort, providing rationale for why things work the way they do, and facilitating problem-solving conversations. The Apprentice needs to feel genuinely seen and supported even while still receiving clear guidance. Research by Dvir, Eden, Avolio, and Shamir in a 2002 longitudinal field experiment on transformational leadership found that individualized consideration — tailoring leadership behavior to each follower's specific needs — was the strongest predictor of follower development. The Developing strategy is, in many ways, the most demanding of the four: it requires a leader to hold both high structure and high relational investment simultaneously.

The third strategy is MENTORING: low directive, high support, designed for the Journeyman. The Journeyman has the skills to do the work but needs the leader to invest in their confidence, motivation, and sense of ownership. The Mentoring strategy reduces directive behaviors significantly — the Journeyman already knows how to do the task and does not need to be told again — while maintaining high supportive behaviors. This means listening more than talking, asking for the person's opinion and input, facilitating their own problem-solving rather than solving problems for them, and providing meaningful rationale that reconnects them to the importance of the work. Maria, our nine-year veteran who has lost motivation since the pastoral change, is the Journeyman archetype: she does not need instruction, she needs a leadership investment in her vision and voice.

The fourth strategy is COMMISSIONING: low directive, low support, designed for the Master. The Master — self-reliant, consistently competent, and intrinsically motivated — is best served by the Commissioning strategy. Both directive and supportive behaviors are reduced, not out of indifference, but out of respect for the person's full development. The Commissioning leader trusts the Master to own the task completely: to set their own timeline, solve their own problems, and pursue the goal without being monitored or managed. The appropriate role of the leader here shifts to that of a resource — available when needed, but not hovering. Premature commissioning, releasing someone before they are ready, is just as damaging as under-commissioning — continuing to direct someone who has long since outgrown the need for direction.

[short pause]

Consider this reflection from a ministry leader captured in the DVULI workbook: "Kim kept coming to me as usual with a million questions. I was feeling frustrated that she didn't just take charge — I know she can do it. Then I realized that even though she is dedicated to the ministry, she hasn't been trained to understand why we do what we do. I've been commissioning when she needs to be mentored or developed for a while." What does this moment of honest self-reflection reveal about the cost of mismatched leadership? And what kind of ongoing attentiveness does the L-S-A require of us?

[short pause]

One of the most significant insights embedded in the L-S-A framework — and one that consistently generates the deepest reflection in DVULI workshops — is the recognition of each leader's most frequently chosen and least frequently chosen strategy. This pattern illuminates what Blanchard, Zigarmi, and Zigarmi in 2013 describe as the DEFAULT STYLE: the leadership approach a leader gravitates toward regardless of what the situation actually calls for.

Default leadership styles are not random. They tend to reflect a leader's own background, personality, and formative experiences of being led. Leaders who were shaped by highly directive environments often default to Instructing. Leaders with relational gifting and a strong desire to empower often default to Commissioning — releasing people prematurely to roles they are not yet ready to carry. Both defaults produce frustration and underperformance, but in opposite directions and for opposite reasons.

The DVULI workbook is explicit about the two most common failure patterns in ministry leadership: mismatched leadership — overseeing a master craftsperson as if they were a novice, or delegating to a novice as if they were a master — is one of the most common and costly mistakes in ministry leadership.

Over-instructing — applying high directive, low support to people who are already skilled and motivated — communicates distrust, stifles initiative, and drives gifted people toward disengagement or departure. It is the leadership equivalent of hovering. Over-commissioning — applying low directive, low support to people who still need structure and encouragement — communicates abandonment. The person feels left to fail, and often does. Both are failures of love, however well-intentioned the leader.

The invitation of the L-S-A is not judgment but awareness: where do I tend to miss? And what might that pattern be costing the people I lead? Returning again to the findings of Dvir and colleagues in 2002 — their rigorous longitudinal study found that leaders trained in individualized consideration produced followers with significantly higher self-efficacy, moral development, and independent performance capacity than control groups. The L-S-A is, at its core, a training instrument for individualized consideration. It builds the diagnostic habit of pausing before leading and asking: Who is this person, on this task, at this moment? And what do they actually need from me right now?

[short pause]

The DVULI framework does not treat the L-S-A as a neutral management tool borrowed from corporate leadership literature and applied to ministry contexts. It situates the entire framework within a specific theological conviction about the nature and purpose of Christian leadership. The anchor text is Second Timothy chapter two, verse two: "And the things you have heard me say in the presence of many witnesses entrust to reliable people who will also be qualified to teach others."

This verse contains an embedded logic of MULTIPLICATION that the L-S-A makes operational. Paul is not simply instructing Timothy to delegate tasks — he is articulating a vision of leadership in which the primary output is not what you accomplish but the multiplicative capacity you build in those you invest in. The key phrase is "qualified to teach others" — this is a Master-level outcome, achieved only when the leader has successfully moved people through Novice, Apprentice, and Journeyman stages by applying the right strategy at each step.

Robert Greenleaf, in his foundational 2002 work on servant leadership, captures the same logic in secular terms. Greenleaf's best test of servant leadership asks whether those being served are growing as persons — becoming healthier, wiser, freer, more autonomous, and more likely themselves to become servants. This is precisely the developmental arc the L-S-A is designed to support. Greenleaf argues that a leader whose followers remain perpetually dependent has failed at the deepest level of leadership, however impressive the leader's own productivity. The goal is not to need to be needed — it is to work toward the point where those you lead no longer need you for the task.

Parris and Peachey's 2013 systematic review of servant leadership research across thirty-nine empirical studies identified developing community, empowering others, and cultivating follower growth as consistent dimensions of servant leader practice — all of which are outcomes of strategic, situation-sensitive L-S-A application. The review further found that the multiplicative development of future leaders — what Second Timothy two-two describes as training those qualified to teach others — is a defining feature of servant leader effectiveness.

This means that the L-S-A is not ultimately about managing performance. It is about loving the people you lead well enough to give them exactly what they need at each stage of their growth — even when that is uncomfortable for you. It is uncomfortable to keep directing someone who bristles at authority. It is uncomfortable to stay engaged supportively with a discouraged Apprentice who pushes back. It is uncomfortable to let go of a gifted Master who no longer needs your involvement. But each of these acts of leadership discomfort is, in the framework of servant love, an act of discipleship.

[short pause]

Everything discussed in this session flows directly into one of the five DVULI Core Values: EMPOWERMENT. As defined in the First National Conference curriculum, the Empowerment Core Value stresses the importance of creating leader-full organizations by exercising servant leadership, allowing others to assume leadership, and developing the skills of those around you. The process embedded in Empowerment is precisely the L-S-A sequence: Identifying, Equipping, Mobilizing, and Supporting.

The L-S-A framework gives operational form to each step of this process. Identifying involves accurate diagnosis of development steps. Equipping is the application of the appropriate strategy — particularly Instructing and Developing for those with low to moderate skill. Mobilizing is the transition to Mentoring, where the leader steps back from direction and invests in the person's ownership and confidence. And Supporting — which maps most directly onto the Commissioning strategy — is the release of a fully developed person into their own sphere of authority and responsibility.

The DVULI Breakthrough Leadership definition — implementing a strategy to empower others for ministry with urban youth — is, from the L-S-A perspective, a Master-level outcome for those we lead. Our job as leaders is to develop the people around us until they are self-reliant enough to implement their own empowerment strategies. That is what it means to build a leader-full organization in the kingdom sense. That is what Paul had in mind when he wrote to Timothy from prison.

The journal entry we encountered earlier captures what it looks like when this insight lands in real ministry life: "I thought I was empowering her by expecting her to take charge, but she isn't ready. I've been commissioning when she needs to be mentored or developed for a while. I'm going to meet with her to work out a plan that will help her develop her skills so that in time she can become an even better leader." This is not just good management. This is the Second Timothy two-two logic coming alive in a Wednesday afternoon conversation with a frustrated volunteer.

[short pause]

Before your next workshop session, reflect on the last month of your leadership practice. For the people you lead on key tasks, which strategy did you most frequently apply — regardless of what those people actually needed? Where do you sense you defaulted? What would it take, practically and emotionally, to shift your approach to match the actual development step of each person? Consider writing a brief journal entry on this as you continue to build your full leadership profile.

[short pause]

Let us draw together the central threads of what we have covered. There is no single best leadership strategy — the best strategy is always the one that matches the development step of the specific person on the specific task. The three L-S-A leader skills are Goal Setting using the SMART framework, Analyzing the development step, and Matching the strategy to the situation. They build sequentially, and each depends on the one before it. Development step is always determined by skill and dedication together, and it is always task-specific — never a global label for a person's worth or capacity. The four development steps — Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, and Master — each call for a different strategy: Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, and Commissioning respectively. Default leadership styles — over-instructing or over-commissioning — are the most common and costly failures in ministry leadership, producing frustration and underperformance in both directions. And the L-S-A framework is theologically grounded in servant leadership and the Second Timothy two-two vision of multiplicative discipleship: our purpose is not to accomplish ministry ourselves, but to develop the self-reliance of those who will carry it forward.

[short pause]

The next session will deepen the conversation about how your own D-I-S-C behavioral style — your natural patterns of dominance, influence, steadiness, and conscientiousness — shapes the way you naturally gravitate toward and away from certain L-S-A strategies. Understanding the connection between who you are and how you lead is the next essential layer in building your full leadership profile as a DVULI participant.

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt:
Create a polished educational infographic in 16:9 landscape format with a light cream background (#faf8f5). Title at the top center in bold dark navy (#00558C) text reading "The Art of the Right Fit: Matching Leadership Strategy to Development Step". Below the title, a subtle tagline in gray italic: "Skill + Dedication = Development Step → Leadership Strategy".

The infographic is divided into four equal vertical columns, each representing one development step and its matched leadership strategy. Each column has a large rounded-rectangle card with a thin top border colored by category.

COLUMN 1 (leftmost, top border #00558C dark): Header label "D1: NOVICE" with a small seedling icon. Below: "Low Skill · High Dedication". A horizontal bar chart showing Skill as a short bar and Dedication as a long bar. Below a downward arrow, a strategy badge reads "INSTRUCTING (S1)". Underneath, two mini indicator bars labeled "Directive: HIGH" (filled bar in #00558C) and "Supportive: LOW" (nearly empty bar in #319B41). A one-line descriptor: "Leader defines the what, when, how."

COLUMN 2 (top border #d97706 warm amber): Header label "D2: APPRENTICE" with a wrench icon. Below: "Low-to-Some Skill · Low Dedication". Bar chart showing both Skill and Dedication as short bars. Arrow down to strategy badge "DEVELOPING (S2)". Indicator bars: "Directive: HIGH" (filled #00558C) and "Supportive: HIGH" (filled #319B41). Descriptor: "Leader coaches and encourages."

COLUMN 3 (top border #319B41 green): Header label "D3: JOURNEYMAN" with a compass icon. Below: "Moderate-to-High Skill · Variable Dedication". Bar chart showing Skill as a long bar and Dedication as a medium bar. Arrow down to strategy badge "MENTORING (S3)". Indicator bars: "Directive: LOW" (short #00558C) and "Supportive: HIGH" (filled #319B41). Descriptor: "Leader collaborates and affirms."

COLUMN 4 (rightmost, top border gold/dark green): Header label "D4: MASTER" with a star icon. Below: "High Skill · High Dedication". Both bars are fully filled. Arrow down to strategy badge "COMMISSIONING (S4)". Indicator bars: "Directive: LOW" (short #00558C) and "Supportive: LOW" (short #319B41). Descriptor: "Leader delegates and trusts."

Across the bottom of all four columns, a horizontal arrow runs left to right labeled "Growth Trajectory" with a gradient from #00558C on the left to #319B41 on the right.

Below the four columns, a footer section with a light #00558C10 background band contains three small icon-and-label clusters representing the three core LSA leader skills: a target icon labeled "Goal Setting (SMART)", a magnifying glass icon labeled "Analyzing (Dev Step)", and a puzzle-piece icon labeled "Matching (Strategy)". These are arranged horizontally and connected by small right-pointing chevron arrows to show the sequential workflow.

Use clean sans-serif typography throughout. All text is crisp and readable. Visual style is flat design with subtle drop shadows on the cards. No photographs. No decorative clutter. The overall feel is professional, modern, and classroom-ready.

config.description: An interactive two-axis grid widget. The horizontal axis represents Skill (low to high) and the vertical axis represents Dedication (low to high), each divided into three zones. Participants click anywhere on the grid to place a dot representing a specific person they lead on a specific task. The widget instantly reveals the development step (Novice, Apprentice, Journeyman, or Master) that corresponds to that skill-dedication combination, the recommended LSA strategy, and a list of the directive and supportive behaviors most useful in that situation. Participants can plot up to five people for five different tasks and see the full spread of strategies their leadership currently requires.
config.rationale: The two-axis spatial grid makes the relationship between skill, dedication, and development step concrete and navigable — participants can literally see how a small shift in skill or dedication changes the recommended strategy, illustrating why situational leadership requires constant reassessment rather than fixed categories.

config.concept: Three LSA leader skills: Goal Setting, Analyzing, Matching
config.description: A timed card-matching game presenting eight ministry scenario cards (e.g., 'Maria has run the youth group for 10 years but has lost motivation since the pastor changed'; 'James just joined your team and is eager but has never led a Bible study') and four strategy cards (Instructing, Developing, Mentoring, Commissioning). Participants drag each scenario to the strategy they believe is the best match within 90 seconds. The widget then scores matches as Excellent, Appropriate, Mismatched-High (over-directing), or Mismatched-Low (under-directing), with explanations of why each match was or was not effective. A summary shows each participant's Matching Success score — the same metric used in the formal LSA report.
config.rationale: The timed constraint prevents overthinking and surfaces intuitive defaults, making dominant strategy biases (e.g., always Instructing) visible in the pattern of mismatches — participants see their own tendencies before they receive the LSA report, creating a 'predict before you peek' moment.

blocks.title: SMART Goal Builder for Ministry Tasks

config.concept: SMART goal setting as the foundation of effective LSA leadership
config.description: An interactive goal-construction widget where participants type a ministry task into a free-text field, then are guided through five toggle prompts — one for each SMART criterion (Specific: Is the task clearly described in observable terms? Motivating: Does this person care about this task? Attainable: Is it realistic given current resources? Relevant: Does it connect to the ministry's mission? Time-Bound: Is there a clear deadline with progress checkpoints?). For each criterion that fails, the widget suggests a specific revision. When all five criteria pass, the widget generates a SMART goal statement and displays it alongside a development-step selector, prompting participants to identify which strategy to apply when launching this goal.
config.rationale: Building a real ministry goal through the SMART scaffold ensures that the abstract framework is encoded through a concrete personal application — participants leave with at least one actionable SMART goal ready for use in their actual ministry, bridging the gap between learning and practice.
